content.full_text:
State of California                                                A-80-157

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration        Executive/Legal          Enforcement/Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                            322-5901                 322-9441


December 25, 1980


Joan Mihay
3485 E. Bellaire Wy.
Fresno, CA 93726

Dear Ms. Mihay:

     This is in response to your letter of November 24, 1980.  Your questions
were as follows:

     1.  Is a $100 check written on the account of John and Jane Doe
         a $50 contribution from each, or a $100 contribution?

     2.  If a husband and wife wish to contribute $250 each, can a
         single $500 check be accepted, or must each write a check
         for $250?

     3.  If a $95 check is first received and then an additional $10
         in cash is received from the same contributor, must that con-
         tributor be identified as giving a contribution of $100 or
         more since our record keeping manual states that cash con-
         tributions under $25 need not be identified by name?

     In answer to your first question, a check written on a joint account
is considered to be a single $100 contribution from both the husband and
the wife and both should be listed as contributors unless there are specific
facts indicating that only one is the contributor.

     Regarding your second question, the Political Reform Act does not limit
the amount that an individual may contribute.  The $250 limitation that you
are referring to may be contained in a local ordinance.  I would suggest that
you contact your local filing official for advice regarding any local ordinances
limiting contributions.

     Your third question asks about record keeping procedures for contri-
butions under $25.  If a contribution for an amount less than $25 is received,
and you are aware that the contribution came from the same individual that
previously contributed and amount greater than $25, then you should cumulate the
two contributions.  The contributor should listed on schedule A of your campaign
statement if the cumulative amount of the contribution is $100 or greater.

     If you have any further questions, please feel free to contact me.


                                          Yours Very Truly,

                                          Lynn Montgomery
                                          Staff Services Analyst

November 24, 1930

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K. Street
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

NOV 23 12:43PM GU

Attn: Technical Assistance
Division

Gentlemen:

I am the Treasurer of a City Council election committee
in Fresno. I understand from reading your information manual
and your record keeping manual that we cannot accept more than
$250 per individual and that we must report the names of all
those contributing $100 or more.

However, I have received conflicting advice on how to
interpret some of your rules, specifically as items under $25
and also the treatment of checks drawn on a joint account.
One person tells me that a $100 check on a joint account is
a $50 contribution from each and need not be identified as the
list of $100 and over. Another ex-treasurer tells that your
auditors regard the signer of the check as the donor.

I would appreciate answers to the following questions:

1. Is a $100 check written on the account
of John and Jane Doe a $50 contribution
from each or a $100 contribution from
one. If from one, which one?

2. If a husband and wife wish to contribute
$250 each, may we accept one check on a
joint account for $500 or must they write
two checks for $250?

3. Your record keeping manual says that cash
contributions under $25 need not be identi-
fied by name. If we have a $95 check from
John Doe and he then gives us $10 in cash,
are we required to list him as having given
more than $100? If so it would seem we do
have to identify all items under $25.

We certainly wish to comply with your accounting and
reporting rules and would appreciate an answer at your
earliest convenience so that we may give the correct information
to our campaign workers.

Sincerely

Dean Mihay
3485 E. Bellaire Way
Fresno, Ca. 93726
(209) 229-2133

P.S. 4. I have just read that the Fresno County ordinance
exempts food and beverages from the $250 limit.
Would that apply to our city election then? Which
laws take precedence- the State or the County.
Would beverage include wine?

Regarding your second question, the Political Reform Act does not limit the amount that an individual may contribute. The $250 limitation that you are referring to may be contained in a local ordinance. Your third question asks about record keeping procedures for contributions under $25. The contributor should listed on schedule A of your campaign statement if the cumulative amount of the contribution is $100 or greater.
